2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 1989 1988 1987 1986 1985 1984 Skip to sidebar Absolutely Positively On Time: What Would It Take? Edward A. Lee Embedded Systems Column, IEEE Computer , July, 2005 Prepublished ve

Column, IEEE Computer , July, 2005 Prepublished version Published version ABSTRACT Despite considerable progress in software and hardware techniques, when embedded computing systems absolutely must meet tight timing constraints, many of the advances

et tight timing constraints, many of the advances in computing become part of the problem rather than part of the solution. Although synchronous digital logic delivers precise timing determinacy, advances in computer architecture and software have m

ances in computer architecture and software have made it difficult or impossible to estimate or predict the execution time of software. Moreover, networking techniques introduce variability and stochastic behaviors, and operating systems rely on bes

astic behaviors, and operating systems rely on best effort techniques. Worse, programming languages lack time in their semantics, so timing requirements are only specified indirectly. In this column, I examine the following question, "if precise ti

, I examine the following question, "if precise timeliness in a networked embedded system is absolutely essential, what has to change?" The answer, unfortunately, is "nearly everything." Contact Update Help Copyright © 1999 - UC Regents; all rights
